card.hook: A browser wallet firewall is only meaningful if it injects on demand with activeTab or explicit host permission and forces every privileged signing request through the content-script message boundary. The moment it falls back to broad host access or opaque raw-sign flows when EIP-712 or permit exists, it recreates the same attack surface it claims to reduce.
card.why_it_matters: If you are learning how browser extensions and smart-contract signing fit together, this gives you a concrete architecture for comparing safer transaction mediation against the permanently injected wallet pattern.
card.action: Write a one-page decision note with two columns, "allow" and "block," listing the exact conditions from the article for approving a signing path: activeTab or explicit host permission present, capability confirmed with wallet_getCapabilities, prefer wallet_sendCalls when bundled calls are supported, prefer ERC-2612 permit over approve, refuse raw-sign when an EIP-712 path exists, and check for address-poisoning patterns before showing approval.
